events: Markets closed Friday Jun 19 (Juneteenth holiday) — today is effectively the last trading day of this shortened week
May PCE inflation + final Q1 GDP revision due Jun 25 at 8:30am ET
US-Iran interim peace deal formal signing anticipated Jun 19 (not yet confirmed)
fed_note: Fed held rates steady at Jun 17 meeting (first under new Chair Kevin Warsh). Dot plot removed prior cut guidance; 9 of 18 officials now project at least one rate hike by end of 2026. Median funds rate projection for end-2026 is 3.8%, implying a hike is on the table.

inflation.note: Inflation materially above the Fed 2% target. Energy prices are the primary driver; US-Iran peace deal could ease this if sustained.

valuation.note: Trailing P/E at top of its 5-year range. Forward P/E modestly above 10-year average. Not screaming cheap.

geopolitical: US-Iran interim peace deal announced; formal signing anticipated Jun 19 (Switzerland). WTI crude fell ~4.8% on the news. Deal reduces near-term energy-price pressure but implementation risk remains — deal not yet fully signed.
market_mood_today: Recovery rally. S&P 500 up ~1.15%, Nasdaq up ~1.5%, after yesterday's Fed-driven selloff. Tech leading. Russell 2000 down 0.72% (small-caps lagging).
key_risks: Fed dot plot signals possible rate hike in late 2026 — higher-for-longer or higher rates compress multiples
CPI at 4.2% and PCE at 3.8% are well above the 2% Fed target; next PCE release Jun 25 could reset expectations again
US-Iran peace deal not yet formally signed; if it falls apart, energy prices spike back and inflation stays elevated
Market trading ~8.4% above its 200-day average and at the top of its 5-year P/E range — limited margin of safety in the index itself
Markets closed tomorrow (Juneteenth) — any bad news tonight has nowhere to go until Monday, reducing liquidity today
Small-caps underperforming today suggests risk appetite is selective, not broad-based
key_tailwinds: SPY is above its 200-day moving average — the primary trend is up, not in breakdown territory
US-Iran peace framework, if it holds, removes the largest driver of energy-price inflation and could open a path for the Fed to pause hikes
WTI crude falling toward $80/barrel reduces input-cost pressure across the economy
Jobless claims came in at 226,000 — near consensus, labor market still healthy
Today's rally is broad in tech — our watchlist names (MSFT, AAPL, GOOGL, NVDA) may benefit
No major macro shock scheduled for today specifically
rationale: The market is bouncing after yesterday's Fed-driven selloff, which is encouraging, but the underlying backdrop has not changed: inflation is 4.2%, the Fed just signaled a possible rate hike (not cut) before year-end under new Chair Warsh, the S&P 500 P/E is near the top of its 5-year range, and our watchlist names are trading above or near fair value. The US-Iran peace deal is a genuine positive for the inflation picture but is not yet formally signed and carries implementation risk. Markets are closed tomorrow, compressing this week to a single remaining trading day. We remain cautious: not panicked (the trend is intact, the economy is not in recession), but demanding a real discount before deploying capital.
note: Today is a green day after a down day — that is not a reason to rush. Our playbook says: buy slowly, demand a margin of safety, hold cash when names aren't cheap. MSFT has a thin 0.26% margin of safety at current prices. V is 11.8% above fair value base. AAPL is deeply above our fair value. None of our three thesis names are at buy-eligible prices today. Hold cash; monitor for better entry levels.
